theory.canonical_statement: t intensity distribution into the required input phase pattern constitutes an inverse problem. The Gerchberg-Saxton algorithm provides an iterative solution by alternately enforcing constraints in real space and Fourier space. The algorithm proceeds as follows. Initialize a complex field $\psi_0 = \sqrt{I_{\text{target}}} e^{i\phi_0}$ with the target intensity and random phase. Then iterate: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\arabic*)] \item Apply forward propagation: $\tilde{\psi}_n = \mathcal{P}[\psi_n]$ where $\mathcal{P}$ represents the helical propagator \item Enforce target intensity constraint: $\tilde{\psi}_n \leftarrow \sqrt{I_{\text{target}}} e^{i\arg(\tilde{\psi}_n)}$ \item Apply inverse propagation: $\psi_{n+1} = \mathcal{P}^{-1}[\tilde{\psi}_n]$ \item Enforce input domain constraints as needed \end{enumerate} Convergence is monitored through the error metric: \begin{equation} \epsilon_n = \frac{\|\,|\mathcal{P}[\psi_n]|^2 - I_{\text{target}}\|_2}{\|I_{\text{target}}\|_2} \end{equation} Iteration continues until $\epsilon_n$ falls below a specified tolerance or a maximum iteration count is reached. The final phase pattern $\arg(\psi_{\infty})$ provides the
theory.technical_interpretation: Relation: ψ_n Arrow sqrtI_target e^iarg(ψ_n) is a symbolic expression, written ψ_n arrow sqrtI_target e^iarg(ψ_n). It introduces a symbol or relation used elsewhere in the framework. It is built from ψ, n, I_. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.
